eraDescription: The early days of Formula 1 focused entirely on raw engine power and mechanical grip rather than aerodynamics. Cars resembled sleek torpedoes or cigars and lacked wings entirely.
engineType: Naturally Aspirated Inline-4, Inline-8, and V6/V8

cars.name: Alfa Romeo 158
cars.description: The Alfa Romeo 158, affectionately known as the 'Alfetta', dominated the very first Formula 1 World Championship season in 1950. Originally designed before World War II, its supercharged engine was incredibly powerful for its time. It remains one of the most successful racing cars ever built.
cars.funFact: Despite its dominance, the car only managed about 1.5 miles per gallon of fuel, requiring massive fuel tanks.

cars.name: Ferrari 500
cars.description: The Ferrari 500 was built to Formula 2 regulations, which the World Championship adopted for the 1952 and 1953 seasons. It was remarkably reliable and handled beautifully thanks to its lightweight 4-cylinder engine. Alberto Ascari drove this car to consecutive world titles with unprecedented dominance.
cars.funFact: Alberto Ascari won nine consecutive championship races in this car, a record that stood for decades.

cars.name: Maserati 250F
cars.description: The Maserati 250F is widely considered one of the most beautiful and iconic front-engined Formula 1 cars ever raced. It was the car that legendary driver Juan Manuel Fangio used to secure his fifth world championship in 1957. The car was beloved by drivers for its predictable handling and long, drifting slides.
cars.funFact: Fangio's drive in the 250F at the 1957 German Grand Prix is often cited as the greatest drive in F1 history.

cars.name: Vanwall VW57
cars.description: The Vanwall VW57 was a pioneering British car that focused heavily on aerodynamic shape to reduce drag, designed by aerodynamicist Frank Costin. It became the first car to win the Formula One Constructors' Championship in 1958. The sleek teardrop body shape made it significantly faster on the straights than its rivals.
cars.funFact: The engine was essentially made by combining four single-cylinder Norton motorcycle engines.

eraDescription: The introduction of aerodynamic wings completely transformed the sport, shifting focus from reducing drag to generating downforce. Cars suddenly grew massive wings and radical shapes as engineers experimented with how to stick the car to the track.

cars.name: Lotus 49
cars.description: The Lotus 49 revolutionized F1 by using the engine as a stressed member of the chassis, saving weight and increasing stiffness. It debuted the legendary Ford Cosworth DFV engine, which would go on to dominate the sport for over a decade. Later versions of the 49 were among the first to experiment with high-mounted wings.
cars.funFact: It won its very first race in 1967 with Jim Clark behind the wheel.

cars.name: Lotus 72
cars.description: The Lotus 72 is one of the most important and successful designs in F1 history, recognizable by its striking wedge shape. It moved the cooling radiators from the nose to the sidepods, improving weight distribution and aerodynamics. Sporting the iconic black and gold John Player Special livery, it raced successfully for six seasons.
cars.funFact: The Lotus 72 was so advanced that it won three constructors' championships over four years.

cars.name: Tyrrell P34
cars.description: The Tyrrell P34 is famous for being the only six-wheeled car to ever race and win in Formula 1. By using four smaller wheels at the front, the designers aimed to reduce aerodynamic drag while maintaining a large contact patch for grip. It was highly distinctive and surprisingly competitive during the 1976 season.
cars.funFact: The smaller front tires required special development by Goodyear and spun much faster than normal tires.

cars.name: Brabham BT46B
cars.description: Known famously as the 'Fan Car', the Brabham BT46B featured a massive fan at the rear driven by the engine. While ostensibly for cooling, the fan sucked air from beneath the car, creating incredible levels of downforce and grip. It was so fast that it won its debut race easily before being voluntarily withdrawn by the team.
cars.funFact: Niki Lauda had to avoid accelerating while stationary, or the massive suction would pull the car down onto the tarmac.

eraDescription: The Turbo era introduced tiny, highly pressurized engines that produced terrifying amounts of horsepower. Cars became brutal to drive, struggling to put over 1000 horsepower down to the road through massive rear tires.

cars.name: Williams FW07
cars.description: The Williams FW07 mastered ground-effect aerodynamics, utilizing skirts to seal the underbody and generate immense downforce. It brought Williams their first World Championships, dominating the grid with its simple yet brilliant design. It was one of the last hugely successful naturally aspirated cars before the turbo revolution fully took over.
cars.funFact: The car was so physically demanding due to the high cornering speeds that drivers often struggled to breathe under the G-forces.

cars.name: Ferrari 126C2
cars.description: The Ferrari 126C2 was a brutally fast and highly capable ground-effect turbo car. It brought Ferrari the 1982 Constructors' Championship thanks to its powerful V6 turbo engine. Sadly, it is also remembered as the car in which the legendary Gilles Villeneuve tragically lost his life.
cars.funFact: The chassis was the first Ferrari to be designed primarily by an English specialist rather than in-house Italian engineers.

cars.name: McLaren MP4/4
cars.description: The McLaren MP4/4 is arguably the most dominant Formula 1 car of all time, winning 15 out of 16 races in the 1988 season. Driven by legends Ayrton Senna and Alain Prost, it perfectly packaged a highly efficient Honda turbo engine into a beautifully low and sleek chassis. It represented the peak of the original turbo era before turbos were banned.
cars.funFact: The driving position was so low that drivers had to lie almost flat on their backs to fit inside the cockpit.

cars.name: Renault RE30
cars.description: Renault pioneered the use of turbocharged engines in F1, and the RE30 was a fast but fragile refinement of their early designs. Alain Prost used the car to win several races, proving the undeniable speed advantage of forced induction. Though unreliable, it paved the way for the turbo-dominated grid of the mid-1980s.
cars.funFact: Renault's early turbo cars were nicknamed 'Yellow Teapots' because they frequently broke down and steamed from their engines.

eraDescription: Computers took over Formula 1 during this era. Teams developed active suspension, traction control, and semi-automatic gearboxes, making the cars incredibly advanced before the FIA banned many of these driver aids.

cars.name: Benetton B194
cars.description: The Benetton B194 carried a young Michael Schumacher to his first World Championship in a highly controversial 1994 season. The car was incredibly nimble and featured an aerodynamically advanced high nose. It remains famous for its distinct green, blue, and yellow 'United Colors' livery.
cars.funFact: The B194 was suspected by rivals of having illegal traction control software hidden deep in its code, though nothing was proven during races.

cars.name: Williams FW14B
cars.description: Often called the most technologically advanced F1 car ever built, the FW14B featured active suspension, traction control, and anti-lock brakes. It completely dominated the 1992 season with Nigel Mansell at the wheel, securing the championship with races to spare. The car's computer-controlled suspension kept it perfectly level through high-speed corners.
cars.funFact: The active suspension required so much computing power that the car essentially carried a heavy, early 90s desktop computer inside.

cars.name: McLaren MP4/8
cars.description: The McLaren MP4/8 was Ayrton Senna's final car for McLaren, featuring advanced active suspension and traction control systems. Despite being underpowered compared to the rival Williams, Senna drove the car to legendary victories, particularly in the wet. It is celebrated as a high-tech marvel that allowed pure driving genius to shine.
cars.funFact: Senna's victory at Donington Park in 1993 in this car features what many call the greatest opening lap in F1 history.

cars.name: Ferrari F310
cars.description: The F310 was Ferrari's first car to feature the roaring V10 engine configuration and was Michael Schumacher's first Ferrari. It suffered from reliability issues and difficult handling but paved the way for Ferrari's future dominance. It is highly recognizable for its raised nose and bulky sidepods, representing a transitional period in F1 design.
cars.funFact: Schumacher famously won the Spanish Grand Prix in torrential rain in this car, lapping almost the entire field despite engine trouble.

eraDescription: Screaming naturally aspirated 3.0-liter V10 engines defined this era, producing over 900 horsepower and an unforgettable ear-splitting sound. Ferrari dominated the period, perfecting aerodynamics and tire performance.

cars.name: Ferrari F2002
cars.description: The Ferrari F2002 is widely considered one of the greatest F1 cars ever made. It was so fast and reliable that Michael Schumacher finished every single race on the podium in 2002, clinching the title in record time. Its seamless gearbox and sculpted aerodynamic shape set the benchmark for years.
cars.funFact: The car was so successful it continued to be used and win races at the start of the following 2003 season.

cars.name: Williams FW24
cars.description: The Williams FW24 featured a monstrous BMW V10 engine that was widely regarded as the most powerful on the grid. While it struggled to match the aerodynamic brilliance of the Ferrari, its raw straight-line speed was unmatched. Driven aggressively by Juan Pablo Montoya, it secured numerous pole positions.
cars.funFact: In this car, Montoya set what was then the fastest qualifying lap in F1 history at Monza, averaging over 161 mph.

cars.name: McLaren MP4/17
cars.description: The McLaren MP4/17 was a competitive but occasionally fragile challenger designed by Adrian Newey. It featured a distinctively low and sharp front nose with the famous West silver and black livery. An updated 'D' version of the car fought closely for the championship in the subsequent 2003 season.
cars.funFact: The car was plagued by unreliability in its debut year, but its fundamental design was so good it was developed into a title contender the next year.

cars.name: Renault R25
cars.description: The Renault R25 ended Ferrari's five-year reign of dominance and brought Fernando Alonso his first World Championship. It was famous for its revolutionary 'mass damper' suspension system, which smoothed out bumps and improved grip. Its screaming V10 engine is widely considered to have produced one of the greatest sounds in motorsport history.
cars.funFact: Fernando Alonso demonstrated the car years later in 2020, and the roaring V10 sound completely captivated the modern paddock.

eraDescription: Engine sizes were reduced to 2.4-liter V8s to slow the cars down. This era saw incredible aerodynamic innovation, resulting in crazy winglets, double diffusers, and exhaust-blown aero that practically glued cars to the floor.

cars.name: Brawn BGP001
cars.description: The Brawn BGP001 is a true fairy-tale car, rising from the ashes of the folded Honda team to win the championship in its only year of existence. It controversially utilized a 'double diffuser' that provided a massive aerodynamic advantage over the competition. Jenson Button drove it to win six of the first seven races of 2009.
cars.funFact: The team was bought for just £1 just weeks before the season started, and the car initially had no sponsors on its bright white livery.

cars.name: Red Bull RB6
cars.description: Designed by Adrian Newey, the Red Bull RB6 was an aerodynamic masterpiece that secured Sebastian Vettel his first world title. It utilized exhaust-blown diffusers to direct hot exhaust gases over the floor, generating massive amounts of downforce. It remains one of the fastest cornering cars in Formula 1 history.
cars.funFact: Mark Webber claimed the car was so planted it felt like it could drive upside down in a tunnel.

cars.name: McLaren MP4/26
cars.description: The MP4/26 featured highly distinctive L-shaped sidepods designed to feed clean air to the rear wing and exhaust area. It was the only car able to consistently challenge the dominant Red Bulls in 2011. Jenson Button used this car to win the famous 2011 Canadian Grand Prix, a race that lasted over four hours due to rain.
cars.funFact: The car was first revealed to the public during a stunt where mechanics assembled it in a public square in Berlin.

cars.name: Ferrari F150
cars.description: The Ferrari 150° Italia was built to celebrate the 150th anniversary of the unification of Italy. Though structurally conservative and struggling with tire temperatures, Fernando Alonso wrestled the car to a remarkable victory at Silverstone. It featured complex front wings typical of the peak aero-focused V8 era.
cars.funFact: Ford sued Ferrari over the original 'F150' name, claiming it infringed on their pickup truck trademark, leading Ferrari to rename it '150° Italia'.

eraDescription: A massive shift in regulations introduced 1.6-liter V6 turbocharged hybrid power units. These incredibly complex engines combine internal combustion with electrical energy recovery, resulting in the most efficient and powerful F1 cars ever.

cars.name: Mercedes W07
cars.description: The Mercedes W07 is statistically one of the most dominant cars in motorsport history, winning 19 of the 21 races in the 2016 season. Its hybrid power unit was vastly superior to the competition in both power and reliability. Nico Rosberg and Lewis Hamilton engaged in a fierce intra-team battle driving this legendary machine.
cars.funFact: The W07 spent roughly 85% of all racing laps in the 2016 season leading the pack.

cars.name: Red Bull RB16B
cars.description: The RB16B finally provided Max Verstappen with the machinery to fight Mercedes for the world title. Powered by a potent Honda engine, it featured extremely tight packaging and advanced aero to claw back downforce lost by regulation changes. It won the thrilling and highly controversial 2021 World Championship.
cars.funFact: To celebrate their engine partnership, the car ran a special white livery in Turkey as a tribute to Honda's first winning F1 car.

cars.name: Ferrari SF90
cars.description: The Ferrari SF90 possessed a famously powerful engine that gave it unmatched straight-line speed throughout the 2019 season. Young star Charles Leclerc used this advantage to claim memorable back-to-back victories at Spa and Monza. It celebrated the 90th anniversary of the Scuderia Ferrari racing team.
cars.funFact: The SF90's engine advantage was later subject to a highly secretive FIA investigation resulting in a private settlement.

cars.name: McLaren MCL35
cars.description: The eye-catching papaya orange MCL35 signaled McLaren's return to consistent competitiveness after years of struggle. Driven by Lando Norris and Carlos Sainz, it proved highly adaptable and secured third in the Constructors' Championship. It featured a distinct matte finish that saved a tiny amount of weight compared to gloss paint.
cars.funFact: In 2021, an updated version of this car gave McLaren their first 1-2 finish in over a decade at the Italian Grand Prix.

eraDescription: To improve overtaking, F1 completely redesigned the aerodynamic rules. Cars now generate most of their grip from underbody venturi tunnels (ground effect) rather than complex over-body wings, allowing them to follow each other much closer.

cars.name: Red Bull RB18
cars.description: The Red Bull RB18 crushed the competition during the dawn of the new ground effect regulations. Despite a heavy start to the season, its exceptional aerodynamic efficiency provided high top speeds while maintaining incredible cornering grip. Max Verstappen secured 15 wins in 2022 alone, breaking the record for most wins in a single season.
cars.funFact: The RB18 won 17 out of 22 races in the 2022 season, cementing its legacy as an engineering masterclass.

cars.name: Ferrari F1-75
cars.description: The F1-75 featured a stunning, aggressive design with unique 'bathtub' style sidepods that caught the imagination of fans globally. It was blisteringly fast in qualifying, taking numerous pole positions in the hands of Charles Leclerc. Though reliability and strategy woes hampered its championship bid, it remained a fan favorite.
cars.funFact: The car's deep red paint was explicitly chosen to celebrate Ferrari's 75th anniversary as a car manufacturer.

cars.name: Mercedes W13
cars.description: The Mercedes W13 shocked the paddock when it arrived with a radical 'zeropod' design, appearing to have almost no side bodywork at all. It struggled massively with 'porpoising', a severe bouncing issue caused by the underbody aerodynamics stalling. Despite the flaws, George Russell managed to secure a memorable victory in Brazil.
cars.funFact: The bouncing was so severe early in the season that drivers reported suffering from headaches and back pain after races.

cars.name: McLaren MCL60
cars.description: Named to celebrate 60 years since Bruce McLaren founded the team, the MCL60 started the season as one of the slowest cars. However, a stunning mid-season upgrade package transformed it into a regular podium contender. The dramatic turnaround proved that mid-season aerodynamic development could still completely change a team's fortunes.
cars.funFact: The car ran a special one-off chrome livery at the British Grand Prix, throwing back to the team's beloved late-2000s look.
